L2_preconditions.label: Pressure for final jump
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states he was 'desperate to get one final jump in before his flight home the next morning.' This time-limited motivation likely increased his willingness to accept conditions that his companions judged unacceptable, contributing to the decision to jump in excessive winds.

L2_preconditions.label: Omitted gear check
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states that neither the jumper nor the nearby partner performed a gear check at the exit. A hand-trace of the bridle routing would have immediately detected the bridle misrouted over the right leg strap that caused the pilot-chute-in-tow.

L2_preconditions.label: Crosswind gust degraded exit
L2_preconditions.description: A strong left crosswind gust caught him immediately after launch, pushing him sideways and back toward the wall. This environmental disturbance directly degraded his body position and contributed to the instability during which he failed to deploy.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized attention/fixation
L2_preconditions.description: After the unstable exit, he focused entirely on regaining a belly-to-earth position and did not deploy the pilot chute. The narrative explicitly states that he fixated on recovering stability instead of pitching, directly enabling the failure to deploy.

L2_preconditions.label: Substance effects
L2_preconditions.description: He had been drinking heavily until 4am, was still visibly impaired on the hike, vomiting, unsteady, and described as 'still wrecked.' This acute alcohol/hangover impairment degraded his coordination and contributed to the unstable exit and subsequent failure to deploy.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient tracking suit experience
L2_preconditions.description: He had bought the tracking suit one month earlier, had only done two skydives in it, and had never jumped it from a cliff. This is far below accepted progression for BASE use of a tracking suit and directly supports his inability to control the suit and generate forward drive after exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Fatigue
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states he drove 14 hours straight through the night and was visibly exhausted at the exit. This fatigue likely degraded his ability to execute precise body control in the tracking suit.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle response
L2_preconditions.description: After the canopy opened 180 degrees off-heading toward the glass facade, she immediately went straight for the toggles and, finding only the right one, pulled it hard. The narrative supports an acute startle-driven reaction to the unexpected imminent collision threat, contributing to the asymmetric control input.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper stated he had done the exit dozens of times and could fly the correct heading 'by feel' despite essentially zero visibility. This indicates he overestimated his ability to compensate for the loss of visual terrain reference.

L2_preconditions.label: Distraction
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper appeared to reach up and adjust his helmet-mounted GoPro during flight. This external task diverted attention from maintaining tracking performance and terrain awareness, directly enabling the loss of separation and failure to correct before impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: Over the prior three months he had at least four near-miss stalls with the same pattern of excessively high angle of attack causing loss of speed and near terrain impact. He explicitly acknowledged his angle-of-attack control was sloppy and said he needed 50 training skydives on stall recovery before more cliff flights, but he never did the remedial training and kept jumping cliffs every weekend.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient wingsuit-specific training
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative directly states he recognized a need to return to the dropzone for substantial additional stall-recovery practice before continuing cliff flights. That uncorrected deficiency in wingsuit flight control training was directly related to the repeated stall pattern and the final fatal stall.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle response/freeze
L2_preconditions.description: Witnesses reported that after opening off-heading toward the guy wires, he made no toggle or riser input, kept his arms at his sides, and appeared to completely freeze as he watched the wires approach. This strongly supports an acute startle/freeze response that prevented the immediate heading correction required.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE experience
L2_preconditions.description: He had about 60 BASE jumps and only two previous antenna jumps, both from taller towers with guy wires farther from the exit point. This limited antenna-specific experience likely reduced his readiness to execute an instant object-avoidance correction in a tighter wire environment.

L2_preconditions.label: Failed to correct known gear defect
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper knew the pilot chute had been torn on the previous jump, was told it needed replacement, and was offered a spare. Despite this clear awareness of a serious equipment deficiency, he chose not to replace the damaged pilot chute before jumping again.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate multi-way briefing
L2_preconditions.description: The group did not properly establish exit order details, separation plan, specific delays, flight paths, or opening altitudes. This inadequate pre-jump coordination created occupied airspace conflicts and directly enabled David's failure to verify clear airspace before deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states he was known for pushing his delays longer and longer over the past season, progressing from 4 seconds to 6 to 8 and sometimes longer. Other jumpers had spoken to him about it multiple times, but he continued the pattern and did not correct this known unsafe tendency.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: He intentionally continued extending delays beyond prior norms despite repeated warnings and despite the narrow altitude margin of the jump. This pattern supports that he overestimated his ability to judge and safely manage increasingly long delays.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient BASE Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had about 40 wingsuit BASE jumps and had only recently transitioned from intermediate suits to an expert-level Aura 1. The narrative explicitly states that expert-level wingsuits have stall boundaries that require many flights to understand and that he had not put in that time, indicating insufficient experience for the specific task attempted.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Knowledge
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative specifically links the fatal stall to limited familiarity with the Aura 1's stall boundaries, noting he had only maybe 10 skydives in the suit before taking it from a cliff. This supports a task-specific knowledge gap about the suit's flight envelope that contributed to the in-flight stall.

L2_preconditions.label: Thermal activity
L2_preconditions.description: The report states the weather was sunny and hot with thermals producing. Thermals can disturb wingsuit flight and may have contributed to the shift in angle of attack that preceded the stall, although the narrative does not establish them as the primary cause.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient skydiving experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 5 skydives and 5 tracking-suit skydives, far below accepted progression norms for any BASE jumping and especially for tracking-suit use. This level of experience is clearly insufficient for executing a stable sub-terminal tracking exit from an earth object and likely contributed to the unstable exit/lack of separation.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of BASE specific training
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had 0 BASE jumps. With no demonstrated BASE experience, the narrative supports that the jumper lacked the BASE-specific training and progression normally required to perform a stable cliff exit and immediate sub-terminal track.

L2_preconditions.label: No effective gear check
L2_preconditions.description: The pilot chute was packed inside the closed container instead of routed to the BOC pouch, a configuration error that an adequate final gear check would likely have detected before exit. This omission did not itself cause the malfunction, but it failed to catch the misrouting that produced the fatal total malfunction.
